meta.title: Cybersecurity
meta.subtitle: defend systems & data
meta.blurb: From foundational security principles and cryptography to application security, network defence, cloud security, and incident response — the knowledge to build and protect secure systems.

t-sec-fundamentals.label: Security Fundamentals
t-sec-fundamentals.desc: Core security principles — the CIA triad, defence in depth, least privilege, and the attacker mindset.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Security protects three things: **Confidentiality** (only authorised access), **Integrity** (data isn't tampered with), and **Availability** (systems stay up). This **CIA triad** is the foundation everything else builds on.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Defence in depth**: multiple overlapping layers (network, application, data, physical) so no single failure is catastrophic. **Least privilege**: grant only the minimum access needed. **Zero trust**: verify explicitly, assume breach. Think like an attacker to defend like a pro.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Security is a **spectrum**, not a binary. Perfect security doesn't exist — you manage risk by making attacks more expensive than the value they'd extract. The weakest link is usually human (phishing, misconfiguration), not cryptographic. Build security into the development lifecycle, don't bolt it on after.

t-threat-landscape.label: Threat Landscape
t-threat-landscape.desc: Who attacks, why, and how — threat actors, attack vectors, and the evolving ecosystem of cyber threats.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Threat actors**: script kiddies, hacktivists, organised crime, nation-states, insiders. **Attack vectors**: phishing, malware, exploits, credential theft, supply chain compromise. Know your adversary to prioritise defences.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **MITRE ATT&CK** maps adversary tactics and techniques across the kill chain: initial access → execution → persistence → lateral movement → exfiltration. Use it to identify coverage gaps in your defences. The **Verizon DBIR** provides annual data on what actually works for attackers.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In 2026, top threats are: **AI-driven ransomware** (RaaS platforms with AI automate up to 90% of intrusions), **agentic attack chains** (AI agents compress patch-to-exploit timelines to minutes), **supply chain attacks** (SolarWinds/xz-utils-style), **multi-extortion** (data theft without encryption), and **cloud misconfigurations** (still the #1 breach vector in cloud). AI-powered phishing uses deepfakes and personalised lures that are nearly indistinguishable from legitimate communications.

t-security-models.label: Security Models & Frameworks
t-security-models.desc: Formal frameworks for thinking about security — NIST, Zero Trust, STRIDE, and threat modelling.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **NIST CSF** organises security into functions: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover. **Threat modelling** (STRIDE, DREAD) systematically identifies what can go wrong before you build. Frameworks give structure to security decisions.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **STRIDE**: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege — one threat per element in your system diagram. **Zero Trust Architecture** (NIST 800-207): never trust, always verify, assume breach.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Threat model at design time — it's 10x cheaper to fix security issues in design than production. Use data flow diagrams, identify trust boundaries, enumerate threats per component. Not every threat needs mitigation — accept, mitigate, transfer, or avoid based on risk appetite and cost.

t-risk-management.label: Risk Management
t-risk-management.desc: Quantifying and prioritising security risks — likelihood, impact, and deciding what to fix first.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Risk = Likelihood × Impact**. You can't fix everything — prioritise by what's most likely to happen and what would hurt most. Security is ultimately about **risk management**, not elimination.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Risk assessment**: identify assets, threats, vulnerabilities, then score. **Risk treatment**: mitigate (fix it), accept (live with it), transfer (insure), or avoid (don't do it). **Vulnerability management**: continuous scanning, CVSS scoring, patch prioritisation.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: CVSS scores alone are poor prioritisers — context matters (is it internet-facing? is there a known exploit? what data is at risk?). Use **EPSS** (Exploit Prediction Scoring System) and **CISA KEV** (Known Exploited Vulnerabilities) to focus on what attackers actually exploit. Communicate risk in business terms, not technical jargon.

t-crypto-basics.label: Cryptography Fundamentals
t-crypto-basics.desc: The mathematical foundations of security — what cryptography provides and its core building blocks.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Cryptography provides: **confidentiality** (encryption hides data), **integrity** (detect tampering), **authentication** (prove identity), and **non-repudiation** (can't deny actions). It's math-based trust in an untrusted world.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Three building blocks: **symmetric encryption** (same key encrypts/decrypts), **asymmetric encryption** (public/private key pairs), and **hash functions** (one-way fingerprints). Everything else (TLS, signatures, passwords) combines these primitives.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Never roll your own crypto — use well-vetted libraries (libsodium, OpenSSL, Web Crypto API). The hard part isn't the math; it's **key management** (generating, storing, rotating, revoking keys securely). **Post-quantum cryptography** is here — NIST finalised standards in August 2024: **ML-KEM** (key encapsulation, replacing Kyber), **ML-DSA** (signatures, replacing Dilithium), and **SLH-DSA** (stateless hash-based signatures). Hybrid key exchange (X25519 + ML-KEM) is rolling out in TLS and Chrome.

t-symmetric.label: Symmetric Encryption
t-symmetric.desc: Encrypting data with a shared secret key — AES, ChaCha20, modes of operation, and authenticated encryption.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Both parties share the **same secret key**. Fast and efficient. **AES-256** is the gold standard. The challenge: how do you securely share the key in the first place?

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Modes of operation** matter: ECB is broken (patterns leak), use **GCM** or **CTR** mode. **AEAD** (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data) — like AES-GCM — provides both confidentiality AND integrity in one operation. Always use AEAD, never bare encryption.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Use **AES-256-GCM** for data at rest and in transit (TLS uses it). **ChaCha20-Poly1305** is the alternative (faster in software without AES hardware). Never reuse nonces with GCM — it completely breaks the security. For disk encryption, use XTS mode.

t-asymmetric.label: Asymmetric Encryption & PKI
t-asymmetric.desc: Public/private key pairs, key exchange, certificates, and the trust infrastructure of the internet.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Two keys: **public** (share freely) and **private** (keep secret). Encrypt with the public key, only the private key can decrypt. This solves the key distribution problem — no shared secret needed.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **RSA** (older, larger keys) and **ECC** (smaller keys, same security). **Key exchange** (Diffie-Hellman, ECDHE) lets strangers agree on a shared secret over an insecure channel. **Certificates** (X.509) bind public keys to identities, issued by **Certificate Authorities** (CAs) in a chain of trust (PKI).

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: TLS combines everything: ECDHE for key exchange, certificates for authentication, AES-GCM for bulk encryption. Use **Let's Encrypt** for free, automated certificates. Prefer **ECDSA** (P-256 or Ed25519) over RSA for new systems. Prepare for **post-quantum**: hybrid key exchange (X25519 + ML-KEM) is rolling out in TLS now.

t-hashing-signing.label: Hashing & Digital Signatures
t-hashing-signing.desc: One-way functions for integrity, password storage, and proving authorship without revealing secrets.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A **hash** is a one-way fingerprint: same input always gives same output, but you can't reverse it. Used for integrity checks, password storage, and signatures. SHA-256 is the workhorse.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: For **passwords**: never use raw SHA-256. Use **bcrypt**, **scrypt**, or **Argon2** — they're deliberately slow, making brute-force expensive. **Digital signatures** (ECDSA, Ed25519) prove a message came from the private key holder without revealing the key.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Use **Argon2id** for password hashing (winner of the Password Hashing Competition). Use **Ed25519** for signatures (fast, secure, misuse-resistant). **HMAC** (keyed hash) provides message authentication when you already share a secret. Never use MD5 or SHA-1 for security — they're broken.

t-secure-coding.label: Secure Coding Practices
t-secure-coding.desc: Writing code that resists attacks — input validation, output encoding, secure defaults, and defensive patterns.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Secure coding assumes **all input is hostile**. Core practices: **validate inputs** (reject unexpected data), **encode outputs** (prevent injection), use **parameterised queries** (never concatenate SQL), and **fail securely** (errors shouldn't reveal system details).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Secure defaults**: deny by default, require explicit allow. **Principle of least privilege** in code: don't request permissions you don't need. Use established libraries for security-sensitive operations (auth, crypto, parsing). Keep dependencies updated — most vulns are in libraries, not your code.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Integrate security into the development workflow: **SAST** (static analysis in CI), **SCA** (dependency scanning), **secrets detection** (pre-commit hooks), and security-focused code review checklists. Shift security left — finding a vuln in code review costs 10x less than finding it in production.

t-web-vulns.label: Web Vulnerabilities (OWASP Top 10)
t-web-vulns.desc: The most critical web application security risks — injection, broken auth, XSS, SSRF, and more.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: The **OWASP Top 10** catalogues the most critical web risks: **Broken Access Control** (#1), **Cryptographic Failures**, **Injection** (SQL, command, LDAP), **Insecure Design**, **Security Misconfiguration**, **Vulnerable Components**, **Auth Failures**, **Data Integrity Failures**, **Logging Failures**, and **SSRF**.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **SQL Injection**: untrusted input in queries (fix: parameterised queries). **XSS**: injecting scripts into pages viewed by others (fix: output encoding, CSP). **CSRF**: forging requests from authenticated users (fix: CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies). **SSRF**: making the server fetch attacker-controlled URLs (fix: allowlists, no internal access).

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Broken access control is #1 for a reason — it's hard to test with automation. Use DAST scanners (Burp Suite, ZAP) for automated finding, but manual pen testing catches logic flaws. Implement **CSP headers**, **CORS properly**, and **security headers** (HSTS, X-Frame-Options). Treat the OWASP Top 10 as a minimum, not a ceiling.

t-auth-authz.label: Authentication & Authorization
t-auth-authz.desc: Proving identity and enforcing permissions — OAuth 2.0, OIDC, JWTs, session management, and access control.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Authentication** (authn): proving who you are (passwords, MFA, biometrics). **Authorization** (authz): what you're allowed to do. These are separate concerns — being authenticated doesn't mean you can access everything.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **OAuth 2.0**: delegation framework (let apps act on your behalf). **OIDC**: identity layer on OAuth (user info, ID tokens). **JWTs**: stateless tokens carrying claims (verify signature, check expiry). **Sessions**: server-side state with cookies (simpler but requires storage). **MFA**: something you know + have + are.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Use a battle-tested identity provider (Auth0, Cognito, Keycloak) — don't build auth from scratch. For authorization: **RBAC** (role-based) for simple apps, **ABAC** (attribute-based) for complex policies, **ReBAC** (relationship-based, like Google Zanzibar) for fine-grained access. Always enforce server-side — client-side checks are cosmetic.

t-supply-chain.label: Supply Chain Security
t-supply-chain.desc: Securing dependencies, build pipelines, and the software supply chain against compromise.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Modern software depends on hundreds of open-source packages. A compromised dependency (malicious update, typosquatting, hijacked maintainer account) can backdoor your entire application. The **software supply chain** is only as strong as its weakest link.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **SCA** (Software Composition Analysis) scans dependencies for known CVEs. **SBOM** (Software Bill of Materials) lists every component. **SLSA** (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) defines build security levels. **Sigstore** provides signing and verification for artifacts.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Pin dependency versions (lock files), scan in CI (Snyk, Dependabot, Trivy), and sign/verify builds. Use **reproducible builds** so anyone can verify the binary matches the source. The 2024–25 xz-utils and polyfill.io incidents showed how sophisticated supply chain attacks have become. Generate SBOMs and attest builds as standard practice.

t-network-security.label: Network Security
t-network-security.desc: Protecting network infrastructure — firewalls, segmentation, IDS/IPS, and encrypted communications.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Network security controls **what traffic flows where**. Key tools: **firewalls** (allow/deny by rule), **segmentation** (isolate sensitive networks), **encryption in transit** (TLS), and **monitoring** (detect anomalies).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Next-gen firewalls** (NGFW) inspect application-layer traffic. **IDS/IPS** (Intrusion Detection/Prevention) look for attack signatures and anomalies. **Network segmentation** limits lateral movement — if one zone is compromised, others stay protected. **mTLS** adds mutual authentication between services.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In cloud environments, traditional perimeter security is dead — use **security groups**, **network policies** (Kubernetes), and **service mesh** (Istio mTLS) instead. Monitor **east-west traffic** (between services), not just north-south (in/out). DNS logging and flow logs are cheap, high-value visibility sources.

t-cloud-sec.label: Cloud Security
t-cloud-sec.desc: Securing cloud environments — IAM, misconfigurations, CSPM, and the shared responsibility model.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Cloud security starts with the **shared responsibility model**: the provider secures the infrastructure; you secure your configurations, data, and access. Most cloud breaches are **misconfigurations**, not provider failures.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **IAM** is the #1 control: least privilege, no long-lived credentials, MFA everywhere. **CSPM** (Cloud Security Posture Management) continuously scans for misconfigurations. **SCPs/Org Policies** prevent dangerous actions at the account level (guardrails).

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In 2026, **CNAPP** (Cloud-Native Application Protection Platform) unifies CSPM + CWPP + CIEM + pipeline security into a single view. Block public S3 buckets and default security groups at the org level. Use **infrastructure as code** scanning (checkov, tfsec, Trivy) to catch misconfigs before deployment. AI-powered CSPM tools now auto-remediate common misconfigurations. Cloud security is 80% IAM and configuration, 20% everything else.

t-container-security.label: Container & Kubernetes Security
t-container-security.desc: Securing containerised workloads — image scanning, runtime protection, network policies, and pod security.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Container security covers the full lifecycle: **build** (scan images for CVEs), **deploy** (enforce policies on what can run), **runtime** (detect anomalous behaviour). Containers share a kernel — isolation is lighter than VMs.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Image security**: use minimal base images (distroless), scan with Trivy/Snyk, run as non-root. **K8s security**: Pod Security Standards (restricted), NetworkPolicies (deny-all default), RBAC (minimal ClusterRoles), secrets encryption. **Runtime**: Falco detects unexpected syscalls, file access, network connections.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Shift left: reject vulnerable images in CI (admission controllers like Kyverno/OPA Gatekeeper). At runtime, monitor for: shell exec into pods, unexpected network connections, privilege escalation attempts. Keep K8s updated — the attack surface evolves fast. Use managed K8s (EKS, GKE, AKS) to reduce control-plane risk.

t-endpoint-security.label: Endpoint & Identity Security
t-endpoint-security.desc: Protecting devices and identities — EDR, MDM, passwordless auth, and phishing-resistant MFA.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Endpoints** (laptops, phones, servers) are where attackers land. **EDR** (Endpoint Detection & Response) monitors behaviour and detects threats post-compromise. **Identity** is the new perimeter — stolen credentials are the #1 initial access vector.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **EDR** (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender) replaces legacy antivirus with behaviour-based detection. **MDM** (Mobile Device Management) enforces security policies on corporate devices. **Passkeys/FIDO2** provide phishing-resistant passwordless authentication.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In 2026, **passkeys** are mainstream for consumer and enterprise auth — phishing-proof by design, with major platforms (Apple, Google, Microsoft) supporting cross-device sync. Combine with device trust signals (is the device managed? updated? compliant?) for conditional access. **XDR** (Extended Detection & Response) correlates signals across endpoints, network, identity, and cloud for faster detection. AI-powered EDR now detects fileless malware and living-off-the-land attacks that evade signature-based tools.

t-recon.label: Reconnaissance & OSINT
t-recon.desc: Gathering information about targets — passive and active reconnaissance, OSINT, and attack surface mapping.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Reconnaissance is the first phase of any assessment: understand the target before testing it. **Passive** (no direct interaction — DNS records, WHOIS, public repos, social media). **Active** (direct probing — port scans, service enumeration).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **OSINT** tools: Shodan (internet-connected devices), Censys, Google dorks, LinkedIn (org structure). **Active**: Nmap (port scanning), directory brute-forcing, subdomain enumeration. Map the full **attack surface**: subdomains, APIs, cloud assets, third-party integrations.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Defenders should run recon against themselves (‘attack surface management’) to find what attackers would find: forgotten subdomains, exposed admin panels, leaked credentials in public repos. Tools like Nuclei, ProjectDiscovery suite automate this. The goal: reduce your visible attack surface before adversaries exploit it.

t-exploitation.label: Exploitation & Penetration Testing
t-exploitation.desc: Actively testing defences by exploiting vulnerabilities — the ethical hacking methodology.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Penetration testing** simulates real attacks against your systems to find vulnerabilities before attackers do. It follows: recon → scanning → exploitation → post-exploitation → reporting. Always with explicit written authorisation.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Web exploitation**: injection (SQLi, XSS, SSRF), broken auth, logic flaws. **Network**: service exploits, default credentials, misconfigurations. **Post-exploitation**: lateral movement, persistence, data exfiltration. Tools: Burp Suite (web), Metasploit (network), custom scripts.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Modern pen testing goes beyond running tools — it's about **chaining** low-severity findings into critical impact (e.g., SSRF + IAM misconfiguration = full cloud takeover). Practice on CTF platforms (HackTheBox, TryHackMe). Red team engagements add social engineering and physical access to the scope. Always communicate findings with clear risk context and remediation guidance.

t-privilege-escalation.label: Privilege Escalation
t-privilege-escalation.desc: Moving from low-privilege access to higher privileges — local, domain, and cloud escalation paths.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: After initial access, attackers escalate privileges: from a normal user to admin/root, or from one service to more sensitive ones. Understanding escalation paths helps you block them.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Local**: SUID binaries, kernel exploits, misconfigured sudo, writable services. **Domain** (Windows AD): Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, delegation abuse. **Cloud**: overly permissive IAM roles, instance metadata (IMDS), cross-account role chaining.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Defend by: enforcing least privilege rigorously, auditing IAM policies with tools (IAM Access Analyzer, ScoutSuite), patching promptly, and monitoring for privilege escalation indicators. In cloud environments, the IAM permission graph IS the attack surface — map and minimise it.

t-social-engineering.label: Social Engineering
t-social-engineering.desc: Exploiting human psychology — phishing, pretexting, and why people remain the weakest link.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Social engineering** manipulates people into breaking security procedures. **Phishing** (fraudulent emails/messages), **pretexting** (fabricated scenarios), and **vishing** (phone calls) bypass all technical controls by targeting the human layer.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Attackers exploit urgency, authority, and trust. **Spear phishing** targets specific individuals with personalised lures. **Business Email Compromise** (BEC) impersonates executives. In 2026, **AI-generated phishing** (deepfake voice/video, personalised at scale) makes detection harder.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Technical controls matter more than training: **phishing-resistant MFA** (passkeys, hardware keys) eliminates credential theft regardless of whether users click links. Add email authentication (**DMARC/DKIM/SPF**), link rewriting, and attachment sandboxing. Awareness training helps but isn't sufficient alone — assume some users will always click.

t-soc.label: Security Operations (SOC)
t-soc.desc: The team and tools that detect, investigate, and respond to security events — SIEM, SOAR, and detection engineering.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A **SOC** (Security Operations Centre) monitors systems 24/7 for security events. Core tools: **SIEM** (aggregate and correlate logs), **EDR** (endpoint visibility), and **SOAR** (automate response playbooks).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Detection engineering**: writing rules/queries that find attacker behaviour in logs. Use **Sigma** (vendor-agnostic detection rules) mapped to **MITRE ATT&CK** techniques. **SOAR** automates repetitive tasks: enrich alerts with context, isolate hosts, block IPs automatically.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Alert fatigue kills SOC effectiveness — tune detections ruthlessly, correlate before alerting, and automate triage. Modern SOCs are adopting **detection-as-code** (version-controlled, tested, CI/CD for detections). AI assistants help analysts summarise alerts and suggest response actions, but human judgement remains essential for novel threats.

t-incident-response.label: Incident Response
t-incident-response.desc: What to do when a breach happens — preparation, detection, containment, eradication, and recovery.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Incident response follows phases: **Preparation** (plans, tools, training) → **Detection & Analysis** (is this real?) → **Containment** (stop the spread) → **Eradication** (remove the threat) → **Recovery** (restore normal) → **Lessons Learned** (improve).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Preparation**: IR plan, communication templates, runbooks, tabletop exercises. **Containment**: isolate affected systems, revoke compromised credentials, block C2 channels. **Recovery**: rebuild from known-good state, validate clean before restoring.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: The #1 incident response failure is **lack of preparation** — teams discover gaps during the crisis. Run tabletop exercises quarterly. Have pre-approved communication templates for legal, PR, and customers. Post-incident reviews should be blameless and focus on systemic improvements, not individual fault. Document timelines meticulously for legal/regulatory needs.

t-forensics.label: Digital Forensics
t-forensics.desc: Collecting and analysing digital evidence — understanding what happened, when, and how.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Digital forensics** recovers and analyses evidence from computers, networks, and cloud environments. The goal: reconstruct what the attacker did, when, and what was affected — while preserving evidence integrity for potential legal proceedings.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Disk forensics**: file system analysis, deleted file recovery, timeline reconstruction. **Memory forensics** (Volatility): analyse RAM dumps for malware, credentials, network connections. **Log analysis**: correlate timestamps across systems to build attack timelines. Cloud forensics: API call logs (CloudTrail), snapshot analysis.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: In cloud environments, traditional disk forensics is less relevant — focus on **API logs**, **container images**, and **network flow data**. Preserve evidence before containment (snapshot VMs, export logs). Chain of custody matters if legal action is possible. Automated forensic collection tools (GRR, Velociraptor) scale across large fleets.

t-compliance-grc.label: Compliance & GRC
t-compliance-grc.desc: Governance, risk, and compliance — meeting regulatory requirements and security standards.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Compliance** means meeting external requirements: regulations (GDPR, HIPAA), standards (ISO 27001, SOC 2), and contractual obligations. It's the minimum bar, not the goal — compliant doesn't mean secure, but non-compliance has real consequences.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **SOC 2**: trust service criteria for SaaS companies (security, availability, confidentiality). **ISO 27001**: international information security management system standard. **GDPR**: EU data protection (consent, data minimisation, breach notification, right to deletion). **PCI DSS**: payment card security.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Automate compliance evidence collection — continuous compliance (Vanta, Drata, Secureframe) replaces point-in-time audits. Map security controls to multiple frameworks simultaneously (one control satisfies SOC 2, ISO 27001, and GDPR requirements). Treat compliance as a **byproduct** of good security, not a separate programme.
